Psalm 90 begins where we must begin: with God's eternity. The psalmist—traditionally thought to be Moses—establishes an absolute reality that should reshape how we see everything else: God alone is our dwelling place through all generations. Not our homes. Not our accomplishments. Not our legacies. God Himself is the only refuge that spans time itself. The God who existed before mountains were born, before the earth took shape, who spoke creation into being from nothing—this God has been and will be the only stable ground for human life. When the psalmist says "from everlasting to everlasting You are God," he is not simply making a poetic statement. He is declaring that God's existence transcends the categories by which we understand time itself. This is meant to dwarf us. And it does.

But then comes the weight of what that eternal God does with temporary humans. He returns us to dust. He sweeps us away in our sleep like grass that springs up in the morning only to fade by evening. He sets our iniquities and secret sins before His presence in unsparing light. The anger and wrath of God against sin—these are not metaphors in this psalm. They are the reality against which all our days are measured. Our seventy or eighty years, if we are strong, are consumed by His fury. Even our pride, the things we work hardest at and care most about, are revealed as "labor and sorrow." The psalmist is not inviting us into comfortable reflection here. He is demanding that we reckon with mortality, judgment, and the actual cost of our rebellion.

Yet the psalm does not leave us crushed beneath this weight. Instead, it moves toward a request so honest it cuts through all pretense: teach us to number our days, that we may gain a heart of wisdom. This is the pivot point. Once we truly grasp that our time is limited, that God's wrath against sin is real, that our works will not endure—only then can wisdom begin. The psalmist then pleads for God to return, to show compassion, to satisfy the servants of the Lord in the morning with His loving devotion. He asks that the days of affliction give way to days of gladness, not because suffering disappears, but because God's favor and work become visible even in the midst of it.

The reflection you hold here is not comfort without honesty. Psalm 90 asks you to stand before the eternal God, to see your sin clearly, to accept that your days are numbered—and then to ask Him to establish the work of your hands. That final request is stunning in its simplicity. After acknowledging that our works are temporary and that our days are few, the psalmist asks God to make those works matter. This is faith without escape routes. It is asking not for extended time or removed consequences, but for God's favor to rest upon us and our labors to be established by Him. That is the only establishment worth having. That is the only way our few years become wisdom rather than futility.
